allergens: milk (non-dairy creamer may contain milk derivatives)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is one sachet (approximately 20 g of powder) dissolved in 150–180 ml of hot water, yielding a ready-to-drink cup of ginger milk tea.

nutrient_source.title: 3-in-1 Ginger Milk Tea — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 3-in-1 ginger milk tea (cr_id D09020004). All per-100g values preserved from the canonical HPB record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for 3-in-1 instant ginger milk tea. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the closest analogous product: instant sweetened milk tea / tea with sugar and milk, which falls in the medium GI range (60–70) per University of Sydney GI Database entries for sweetened tea beverages. The dominant glycemic driver is sucrose (6.75 g/100g) diluted in a milk-tea matrix. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (65) × available carbohydrate per serving (10.17 g/100g × 20 g serving = 2.034 g) / 100 = 1.3 GL units.

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar content (6.75 g/100g) in a sugar-sweetened beverage independently raises blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines, despite very low sodium (20 mg/100g).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this sugar-sweetened beverage and instead brew fresh ginger tea with unsweetened low-fat milk, as the AHA identifies regular SSB consumption as an independent risk factor for elevated blood pressure regardless of sodium content.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 6.75 g sugar/100g and low dietary fibre (0.37 g/100g) causes rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation; ADA guidelines classify all SSBs as unsuitable for Type 2 Diabetes management.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this instant premix entirely, as the liquid sugar matrix is rapidly absorbed and the negligible fibre content provides no glycaemic buffering; a suitable alternative is freshly brewed ginger tea with unsweetened milk and no added sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Non-dairy creamer typically contains palm-oil-derived saturated fat (estimated ~1.8 g saturated fat/100g), and the added sugar load (6.75 g/100g) contributes to dyslipidaemia and endothelial inflammation risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk should limit consumption of this product due to its palm-oil-based saturated fat from the non-dairy creamer and its added sugar content, both of which are associated with adverse lipid profiles and increased CVD risk per AHA dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sugar content (6.75 g/100g) and palm-based saturated fat in non-dairy creamer are pro-inflammatory, negating the anti-inflammatory gingerol benefit of the ginger extract.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should brew fresh ginger tea with unsweetened milk to obtain gingerol anti-inflammatory benefits without the pro-inflammatory sugar and palm-oil saturated fat present in this instant premix.

llm_training.natural_language_description: 3-in-1 Ginger Milk Tea is an instant beverage premix popular in Singapore and Southeast Asia, sold in individual sachets that combine tea powder, non-dairy creamer, sugar, and ginger extract in a single convenient format. The '3-in-1' designation refers to the three pre-combined components — tea, creamer, and sugar — requiring only the addition of hot water to prepare. The resulting drink is warm, mildly spicy from the ginger extract, lightly sweet, and creamy from the non-dairy creamer, which is typically palm-oil-based. Per 100 g of prepared beverage, it provides 60 kcal, 0.46 g protein, 1.96 g fat (predominantly saturated from palm-oil creamer), 10.17 g carbohydrate, 6.75 g sugar, 0.37 g dietary fibre, and 20 mg sodium. The glycemic index is estimated at 65 (medium), driven primarily by the sucrose content. Clinically, this product is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) and is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension due to its added sugar load. The ginger extract provides trace gingerols but insufficient quantity to offset the metabolic impact of the sugar and saturated fat. It is commonly consumed at breakfast or as a morning warm drink in Singapore, Malaysia, and the broader Southeast Asian region. The product is ultra-processed and should be consumed in moderation as part of a balanced diet.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a sachet of ginger milk tea?
Can diabetics drink 3-in-1 ginger milk tea?
Is ginger milk tea good for blood pressure?

Is instant ginger milk tea suitable for weight loss?
Does ginger tea help with inflammation?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry (cr_id D09020004) with confidence 0.95. The food is an exact match in the primary regional source. GI is an analogous estimate (medium confidence for GI component only) as no direct GI measurement exists for this specific product.
notes: 1. Saturated fat estimated at 1.80 g/100g based on the typical palm-oil non-dairy creamer composition in 3-in-1 instant tea products; the HPB source does not separately report saturated fat for this entry. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient, and moisture data for this instant ginger milk tea premix are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry D09020004 as of 2026. 3. calcium_mg and iron_mg are reported as 0.0 in the HPB source (confirmed zero per HPB Foodo D09020004). 4. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on sweetened instant milk tea entries in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this specific product was found. 5. Glycemic load per serving calculated using 20 g serving size: GL = 65 × (10.17 × 0.20) / 100 = 1.3. 6. Halal status assumed based on typical commercial production in Singapore; consumers should verify product-specific halal certification. 7. The dietary tags 'low_sodium' and 'low_calorie' from the input are preserved as contextually accurate (20 mg sodium/100g; 60 kcal/100g) but clinical suitability for disease conditions is governed by the health_context section.
